entry.title: How to Add Additional Currency Fields to the Cost Centers Review Booklet
entry.content:
In this blog, we want to illustrate how to use data source extensions in the Custom Fields (F1481) app to add additional currency fields such as functional and freely defined currencies to the Cost Centers Review Booklet (F7763). The standard settings in the Cost Centers Review Booklet expose the global and company code currency. 

Understanding the Data Source Architecture 

Before diving in, it’s important to understand the underlying data model. The Cost Centers Review Booklet is built on CDS analytical queries that sit on top of a shared cube: 

Layer 

CDS View Name

Used For 

Query 

Cost Center Actuals - Query (C_COSTCENTERACTUALQUERY) 

Actuals pages 

Query 

Cost Center Plan and Actual Values - Query (C_COSTCENTERPLANACTUALQUERY) 

Plan/Actuals pages 

Cube 

Cost Center Review Booklet - Cube (I_ACTPLNCOSTCENTERRPTGCUBE) 

Underlying cube for both queries 

This two-layer architecture is critical because it determines where you need to create your data source extension: 

If the field is available in the CDS query but simply not visible in the Cost Centers Review Booklet, you only need to create a data source extension directly on the query.  

If the field is not available in the CDS query but exists in the underlying cube, you need to create a data source extension on the cube, publish it, then create a second one on the query. 

Key Rule: For cube-based analytical queries, always extend the cube first, then the query. 

Additional currency fields such as functional and freely defined currency are available in the CDS query. Therefore, we will focus on data source extensions on the query in this blog. 

Step-by-Step Procedure 

Step 1: Verify the Required Currency Fields Are Not Yet Available 

Before making changes, confirm that the currencies you want to add are not yet available in the Cost Centers Review Booklet. 

  1. Open the Cost Centers Review Booklet app. 
  2. Set your filter parameters (for example, company code 1010) and choose Go.
  3. Open the Layout section in the side panel and check the available currencies. 

 

 

Step 2: Create a Data Source Extension on the CDS Query 

  1. Open the Custom Fields app. 
  2. Switch to the Data Source Extensions tab.
  3. Choose Create. 
  4. Select the Data Source Cost Center Actuals – Query (C_COSTCENTERACTUALQUERY) if you want to add the dimension on Actuals pages or Cost Center Plan and Actual Values - Query (C_COSTCENTERPLANACTUALQUERY) if you want to add the dimension on Plan/Actuals pages. 
  5. Enter an Extension ID (for example, YY1_CURRENCYREVIEWBOOKLET) and a meaningful description. Then, choose Create.
  6. In the Available Fields panel, search for the currencies you need.
  7. Choose Save, then Publish. 

Tip: Use the field search (magnifying glass icon) to quickly look up data source fields. You can switch between technical and human-readable names using the toggle in the upper right corner. 

 

Step 3: Display the Added Currencies in the Cost Centers Review Booklet 

Now, verify that the currencies appear in the app: 

  1. Open the Cost Centers Review Booklet app. 
  2. Set your filter parameters and choose Go. 
  3. Open the Layout section in the side panel and check for the added currencies in the Available Objects list under Measures.

The additional currency fields now appear in the report and can be used for analysis. 

 

Further Reading 

Creating Data Source Extensions 

Cost Centers Review Booklet 

Center Actuals - Query (C_COSTCENTERACTUALQUERY) 

Cost Center Plan and Actual Values - Query (C_COSTCENTERPLANACTUALQUERY) 

Cost Center Review Booklet - Cube (I_ACTPLNCOSTCENTERRPTGCUBE) 

 

Do you have questions or feedback? Feel free to leave a comment below or contact us on the SAP Community!

entry.title: Whether the SAP EWM module supports type B-Customer Sock inbound dn and inventory?
entry.content:
1. The SAP testing system creates a type B goods inboound DN ( B- Customer Stock), with the storage location being an EWM-enabled location, and then send to  EWM system.
2. EWM monitor /N/SCWM/MON queue monitoring, the goods receipt document is stuck in the queue due to an error: "Error during mapping: material cc03040002003 does not exist," Message ID /SCWM/ERPINTEGRATION, Message No 088.
3. Checking the detailed error log of the queue shows: "Use of customer stock not alowed for disposal party 8181 and product
CC03040002003."Message class /SCWM/MD.
4. l would like to ask whether the SAP EWM module supports type B-Customer Sock inbound dn and inventory. If supported, how should I resolve this queue blockage caused by the error?

entry.title: Re: Building and Deploying Your First MCP Server on SAP BTP
entry.content: Hi @AmitBangotra , that is valid feedback that you share. But in order to use MCPs in Integration Suite there is no need for Edge Integration Cell. The new API Centric integrations via Integration Cell make that possible in the cloud. (see https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/api-centric-integration-in-sap-integration-suite-a-new-paradigm-for-api-amp/ba-p/14438245 and https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/mcp-gateway-in-sap-integration-suite-your-apis-ready-for-the-age-of-agents/ba-p/14438250). This allows you to enable MCPs on top of SAP APIs without any custom development. Beyond the Basic/OAuth authentication in the current release, additional SSO concepts (SAML and principal propagation) are on the roadmap to be used for MCP consumption.

entry.title: Re: Identify Change Documents for Purchase Order in Output Determination
entry.content: You can achieve this by using the **Change Output** functionality for Purchase Orders. Configure separate Output Parameter Determination rules for the initial PO and changed POs, and maintain different recipients and scheduling options accordingly. The exact available condition may depend on your S/4HANA version and output management framework.

entry.title: Re: Need to get CDS View and Dataproduct Name
entry.content: Hi, you can find the available CDS Views and Data Products in the SAP Datasphere Business Content documentation. For integration, it is recommended to use released standard CDS Views rather than directly accessing backend tables. The underlying tables depend on the specific CDS View. If you share the business area or Data Product name, we can identify the exact CDS View and source details.

entry.title: Re: Building and Deploying Your First MCP Server on SAP BTP
entry.content: Great explanation! MCP is essentially creating a standardized bridge between AI models and external tools, data sources, and APIs. The USB analogy makes the concept very easy to understand—build the integration once and make it reusable across different AI applications.

entry.title: Re: SAP Business One – Cross Business Partner Down Payment Reconciliation (Same Vendor, Different Br
entry.content: In SAP Business One, a down payment and AP invoices belonging to different BP CardCodes generally cannot be directly reconciled through standard internal reconciliation. The recommended approach is usually to transfer the balance from XXX1 to XXX2 through an appropriate journal entry or BP-to-BP reclassification, subject to your company's accounting and tax requirements. However, this should be carefully evaluated with your finance/tax team, especially if the vendors are legally the same entity. A better long-term solution may be to maintain a single BP master with multiple addresses/locations, if the business and statutory requirements permit.

Looking forward to seeing how the ADT MCP server evolves in future releases!

entry.title: Re: SSCUI 101518 (Define Automatic Credit Control): Can C...
entry.content:
Hello @byd 

Thank you for your question

 

The Currency and Fiscal Year Variant displayed in configuration activity, Define Automatic Credit Control, are attributes of credit control area 1000. They are not maintained separately for each risk category or credit group in this activity.

Consult the following reference for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/918bca53037f408f91a2295d04ac16bc/d91d2a347d0245fab15bdc42c88bd3dc.html?locale=en-US

Question: 1. Is it possible to set a currency other than USD (in our case JPY)? If yes, is it maintained in this SSCUI, or in a different configuration activity (e.g. credit segment / credit control area definition)?
If it is not changeable, what is the impact of leaving it as USD when the company code currency is JPY?

Answer: You cannot directly change the delivered currency USD in Define Automatic Credit Control. This activity controls the credit checks and system reaction for combinations of credit control area, risk category, and credit group.

You do not need the credit control area currency to be the same as company code currency. If the company code posts in JPY while the credit control area is in USD, credit relevant document values are translated for credit exposure and comparison. Therefore:

  •  Your Sales documents and receivables can remain in JPY
  • You can evaluate Credit exposure, and limits are in the relevant Credit Management currency.
  • Exchange rate movements can cause the translated USD exposure to change.
  • It is important you maintain credit limits and interpret them consistently in the currency displayed for the credit segment or credit account.

Ensure you do not change the company code currency or Primary finance settings merely to align this displayed value.

In current Credit Management, the operational credit limit and exposure are managed at Business Partner and credit segment level. The credit control area entry in 101518 mainly links SD processing to the configured credit check. The system reaction for SD credit checks is maintained in Define Automatic Credit Control.

Consult the following reference for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/918bca53037f408f91a2295d04ac16bc/4d6c25bcfc6d44b4e10000000a42189e.html?locale=en-US

 

Question 2: Is the Fiscal Year Variant configurable here? If yes, where is it maintained? If it is inherited/read-only, please advise which object it is derived from.

Answer: K4 is also not maintained per risk category in SSCUI101518. It is inherited from the underlying organizational and finance setup and is displayed with the credit control area data.

For your primary ledger, the fiscal year variant is selected during Specify Primary Finance Setting in CBC. The selected primary fiscal year variant you selected applies across the system and cannot be changed after activation.

Consult the following reference for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/ab27e4be7cb04e4bb292dff2987f25ba.html?locale=en-US

 

Therefore, it is important you do not maintain a separate fiscal year variant for credit control area 1000. You can assign an alternative fiscal year variant only in supported non leading ledger scenarios and does not redefine the fiscal year variant shown for SD automatic credit control.

Consult the following reference for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/9dda2d38e0e74490a99dcaf5b5c678f3.html?locale=en-US

Here are areas you can consider:

  • In SSCUI 101518, ensure you confirm that the Currency and FYV are read only.
  • Then you can review the customer in the credit management application and verify the credit segment, credit limit currency, current expose, and exchange rate conversion from JPY.

 Let me know if you have any further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Multiple/concurrent employment in Compensation Module
entry.content:
Multiple/concurrent employment - can the system natively bring the part-time contract's salary onto the primary contract's row, to be used as the calculation base for Compensation/Variable Pay?

We're implementing Compensation and Variable Pay with EC integration. We have employees with concurrent employment - a primary contract plus a secondary/part-time one.

Confirmed behavior: each contract shows up as its own separate row on the worksheet, with its own salary, pay grade, and independently calculated increase/bonus. The two rows aren't aggregated - if the two contracts have different managers, budget and approval are handled separately per row; if they share the same manager, both rows land on the same worksheet, but still calculated independently.

Questions:

  1. Is it technically possible, natively, for the part-time contract's salary to be brought onto the primary contract's row, so it can be used as part of the calculation base for that employee's increase/bonus?
  2. Is it possible for the compensation result to be published only on the primary contract, rather than on both contracts/rows?
  3. Is it possible for the secondary contract's row to show 0 across all components (Merit, Lump Sum, and any other component), so no increase is applied there?

Has anyone achieved any of this out of the box, or are they simply not supported by design?

Thanks!

entry.title: Consolidation financial statements from Group reporting
entry.content:
Hello

In the financial consolidation, we don't include the financial statements of consolidation units which are acquired for less than 50% (those with equity method). We only add good will postings in case of first consolidation and current profit adjustment in case of subsequent consolidation.

My question is - is there any report which gives the real consolidated financial statement , that is, excluding those of units with equity method, in group reporting ? One way of getting this report is to run  Group data analysis with only consolidation group which do not include units with equity method. As COI postings are made at Investor unit, this approach will work. But I am looking for a report which excludes such units and provide the real consolidated financial statement, because we don't want create another consolidation group for reporting purpose and would like to use the same group with which we do the data and consolidation monitor tasks.

Appreciate your guidence.

Regards,

Rama

entry.title: From Store Visit to Store Performance: Store KPIs in SAP Sales Cloud V2 (build on Surveys)
entry.content:
Every field sales rep knows the frustration: a product is out of stock, a promotion is missing, or shelf space has been lost — and nobody knew until it was too late.

Imagine SunSip Beverages. SunSip Beverages is a global beverage company with field sales reps visiting retail accounts across markets. Their key challenge is : ensuring products are on the shelf with optimum share of shelf and promoted correctly — at every store, every visit. For this they track three key KPIs: On Shelf Availability, Share Of shelf and Promotional Compliance. 

These KPIs provide insights to the Sales Rep while working on an intense Store Audit Process. In SAP Sales Cloud V2, KPIs derived directly from survey responses give sales rep an objective, data-driven view of how well the sales team and their stores are performing (availability, compliance, share).​

First Step: Build the Survey and Survey KPI

As a first step, survey designer will build the survey by including questions that will help us to capture the key information related to these KPIs. Example - three Quantity questions were added to capture the Share Of Shelf which pertain to number of our facings, total number of facings and share of shelf. Similar questions are created for Promotional Compliance and On shelf availability. 

Once the survey is configured, survey designer will proceed to create Survey KPIs. Survey KPIs gives you the capability to define your KPI formula and the RAG thresholds. The formula editor is fully keyboard-navigable, accessing the questions of survey on the right hand side pane and also an easy keyboard friendly edit mechanism. 

For accessing the different functions provided, use $ to see the complete list. Use { to access all the questions and their responses. Click anywhere to start editing. 

Second Step: Associate the Survey KPIs to Visit via Activity Plan

Survey KPIs should become a key requisite in the way we plan our Visits. It also becomes a tool for Sales Rep to prepare of their Visit understanding the key KPIs information. In the Activity Plan, associate your key KPIs and Surveys. A dedicated selector is provided which lets the user pick all the Active KPIs in the system. The user can also filter based on the choice of their survey. 

Third Step: Fill the survey responses to capture KPIs

This is where it all comes together.  

First, as the sales rep enters the store, they are quickly prepared by viewing the key KPIs which need to be captured. Users can preview the KPIs of the visit, the last score and the RAG thresholds. Once prepared, they check in and proceed to take the survey. Once the product survey is taken, the KPIs are automatically calculated and notified to the user. 

Some of the key benefits of the functionality is:

  • Measure sales execution quality — KPIs derived directly from survey responses give sales rep an objective, data-driven view of how well the sales team is executing in the field (compliance, availability, share).​
  • Visual performance tracking via thresholds — The 3-level color-coded threshold lets you instantly see whether a KPI is on track, at risk, or failing — without needing to dig into raw survey data.​
  •  Flexible formula builder — The combination of COUNT, SUM, AGGRWT functions with full arithmetic and logical operators lets you build tailored KPIs for very specific business scenarios.

Survey KPIs in SAP Sales Cloud V2 transform the store visit from a subjective walkthrough into a structured, measurable execution checkpoint. For SunSip Beverages, this means every rep walks in knowing exactly what to look for, captures it consistently, and walks out with a score that reflects reality — not gut feel. Over time, these scores compound into a clear picture of execution quality across markets, accounts, and teams, giving sales leaders the data they need to coach, prioritise, and act.

If you are exploring how to bring more structure and accountability to your field sales execution, Survey KPIs are worth a close look. Try it out and share your experience in the comments below — we would love to hear how your team is measuring what matters in the field.

entry.title: Re: Prevent cross client transports imported multiple tim...
entry.content:
Thanks Richard (and sorry for the long time since coming back). <This is RIDICULOUS. there is only a single line of comment>

Only now when I later edit this, I get a proper windows!!!

---

Anyways I digress...

What we actually are dowing now is to use the Target Group also in DEV. This works well for Customizing and Workbench. Repo and DDIC *will not* be imported again actually even if the TRs are in the import queue of the second client. (We learned that this feature was more intended to speed up performance and we utilise it in our setup to not having REPO and DDIC imported a second time).

We struggled somewhat in filtering the Import Queue though. As we are using Retrofit, we end up with some (kind of intermediary) ToCs when the retrofit was performed. (We use Target Request mode). Those ToCs we would not want to import and filtered them out by target client

---

About your original suggestion using SCC1N: How good of a tool is it? Our Basis is quite fond of their TMS and SCC1N was deemed to be more in the developer area. We might switch to SCC1N for the DEV environment if it is worth the while

Cheers

J

entry.title: Discontinuation Indicator 1 works in MD04 but not in Production Order
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I have configured Material A with Discontinuation Indicator = 1, maintained a discontinuation date, and assigned Material B as the follow-up material.

The behavior I observe is:

  • In MD04, the discontinuation works as expected. I can see that the available stock of Material A is consumed first, and once it is exhausted, the remaining requirement is switched to Material B.
  • However, when I create a production order, the substitution does not occur. The production order still contains Material A as the component, even when there is not enough stock of Material A to cover the required quantity. The system does not automatically replace it with Material B.

entry.title: In-Period and Period-End Forecasting based on Actuals
entry.content:
In this blog, In-Period Forecasting and Period-End Forecasting based on Actuals along with examples is explained. Before proceeding, please refer to the blog Forecasting in Commercial Project Management.

The following forecasting key figures are calculated in this blog:

  • Estimate at Completion (EAC)
  • Estimate to Complete (ETC)

Note: Key Figure values from all approved Change Requests will be considered and calculated as Plan Key Figure values. In this blog, Key Figure represents cost, revenue, and quantity based on resource type and forecast basis. Examples are focused on cost and quantity; however, revenue calculation is similar to cost except that commitments are not considered.

Overview: What's the Difference?

 

Aspect In-Period ForecastingPeriod-End Forecasting
Forecast Period ETCCompares Actual vs Plan; ETC = Plan − Actual (if Plan > Actual), else 0Always 0
RevenueNot applicableForecast for Revenue is also calculated

Calculation Logic

Let us consider a financial plan across multiple periods. The calculation for each period is done based on the following logic:

When Forecasting has NOT been executed before

 

Period In-Period ForecastingPeriod-End Forecasting
Before Forecast PeriodETC = 0ETC = 0
Forecast PeriodETC = 0 if Actual ≥ Plan; ETC = Plan − Actual if Plan > ActualETC = 0
After Forecast PeriodETC = PlanETC = Plan

 

Note: Once Period-End Forecasting has been executed for a period, In-Period Forecasting can no longer be performed for that period. In-Period Forecasting will become available again when the next period opens.

When Forecasting HAS been executed before

 

Period In-Period ForecastingPeriod-End Forecasting
Before Forecast PeriodETC = 0ETC = 0
Forecast PeriodETC = 0 if Actual ≥ Plan; ETC = Plan − Actual if Plan > ActualETC = 0
After Forecast PeriodETC remains unchanged from the last forecast runETC remains unchanged from the last forecast run

EAC Formulas (applicable to both)

  • EAC Quantity = Total ETC Quantity + Total Actual Quantity (up to Forecast Period).
  • EAC Cost = Total ETC Cost + Total Actual Cost (up to Forecast Period) + Total Commitments (up to Forecast Period).
  • EAC Revenue (Period-End only) = Total ETC Revenue + Total Actual Revenue (up to Forecast Period)

For quantity-driven Resource Types: if Rate (Forecast) of the current period is null, Rate (Cost) of the current period is used as the Forecast Rate.

Example

Consider a Financial Plan with a monthly breakdown from January to June for Resource Type Activity with plan/forecast basis set to Quantity.

Planned and Actual Quantities:

 

Month Planned Quantity (H)Actual Quantity Posted (H)Rate (Cost) Cost (Plan) (EUR)
January10080101000
February2000102000
March3000103000
April4000104000
May5000105000
June6000106000

First Forecast Run — Forecast Period: January (Actual = 80H)

For January (Forecast Period):

 

ETCIn-Period ForecastingPeriod-End Forecasting
Quantity (H)20 (Plan 100 > Actual 80 → ETC = 100 − 80)0 (always 0 at period-end)
Cost (EUR):  ETC Quantity * Rate20 × 10 = 2000 × 10 = 0

For February to June (Periods after Forecast Period):

Both In-Period and Period-End forecasting yield the same results here as Actual = 0 for all future periods:

 

Month ETC Quantity (H)ETC Cost (EUR): ETC Quantity * Rate
February2002000
March3003000
April4004000
May5005000
June6006000

ETC and EAC Summary — First Run:

 

CalculationIn-Period ForecastingPeriod-End Forecasting
Total ETC Quantity20+200+300+400+500+600 = 2020 H0+200+300+400+500+600 = 2000 H
Total Actual Quantity (upto Jan)80 H80 H
EAC Quantity2020 + 80 = 2100 H2000 + 80 = 2080 H
Total ETC Cost20200 EUR20000 EUR
Total Actual Cost (upto Jan)800 EUR800 EUR
Total Commitments00
EAC Cost20200 + 800 + 0 = 21000 EUR20000 + 800 + 0 = 20800 EUR

Second Forecast Run — Same Forecast Period (January), Additional Actuals Posted (Actual = 150H)

Updated Data:

Month Planned Quantity (H)Actual Quantity Posted (H)Rate (Cost) Cost (Plan) (EUR)
January100150101000
February2000102000
March3000103000
April4000104000
May5000105000
June6000106000

For January (Forecast Period):

 

ETCIn-Period ForecastingPeriod-End Forecasting
Quantity (H)0 (Actual 150 > Plan 100)0
Cost (EUR):  ETC Quantity * Rate00

For February to June:

 

ETC (Feb–Jun)In-Period ForecastingPeriod-End Forecasting
 Quantity (H)Retained from last forecast run (200, 300, 400, 500, 600)Retained from last forecast run (200, 300, 400, 500, 600)
Cost (EUR): ETC Quantity * RateRetained from last forecast run (2000, 3000, 4000, 5000, 6000)Retained from last forecast run (2000, 3000, 4000, 5000, 6000)

Both In-Period and Period-End forecasting yields the same ETC values for future periods in this run.

ETC and EAC Summary — Second Run:

 

Calculation In-Period ForecastingPeriod-End Forecasting
Total ETC Quantity0+200+300+400+500+600 = 2000 H0+200+300+400+500+600 = 2000 H
Total Actual Quantity (upto Jan)150 H150 H
EAC Quantity2000 + 150 = 2150 H2000 + 150 = 2150 H
Total ETC Cost20000 EUR20000 EUR
Total Actual Cost (upto Jan)1500 EUR1500 EUR
Total Commitments00
EAC Cost20000 + 1500 + 0 = 21500 EUR20000 + 1500 + 0 = 21500 EUR

Both methods converge to the same EAC values in the second run because actual postings exceeded the plan for January — removing the only difference between the two approaches.

entry.title: Re: How are you handling the reconciliation of opening su...
entry.content:
If I am correct, 561 O uploads subcontracting stock balances but does not generate GST challan references. J1IGRECON reconciliation logic is designed to match 101 GRs (with 543 O consumption) against specific challan numbers. Without those challan references, the system cannot perform line‑item reconciliation, which is required for the 180‑day ITC compliance check.
Having said that, there is no standard automated way to reconcile bulk 561 O stock against dozens of legacy challans in J1IGRECON. The best practice is to simulate challans during migration (either dummy challans or split uploads) so that the reconciliation engine has valid references. Otherwise, you need a custom mapping enhancement.

entry.title: COHV Availability Check clears MSPT but does not update RESB-VMENG after COGI recovery
entry.content:
Hello,

I have a question regarding the behavior of the Collective Availability Check (COHV) in SAP S/4HANA.

System

  • SAP S/4HANA 2023

Scenario

  1. A production order is released.

  2. During confirmation, a component cannot be backflushed because of insufficient stock.

  3. The production order receives the MSPT (Missing Parts) status.

  4. After sufficient stock becomes available, the failed goods issue is successfully recovered using COGI. The component is fully backflushed, but the MSPT status remains.

  5. The production order is then confirmed and delivered, resulting in the statuses CNF, DLV, and MSPT.

  6. The affected component is neither a phantom assembly nor a bulk material.

  7. In table RESB:

    • Withdrawn quantity = 1

    • Confirmed quantity (VMENG) = 0

  8. I execute the Collective Availability Check in transaction COHV.

Result

  • The MSPT status is cleared.

  • However, RESB-VMENG remains 0 and is not updated.

Question

According to SAP Note 423538, VMENG is updated by the availability check.

Therefore, I expected that executing the Collective Availability Check in COHV would also update RESB-VMENG after the missing part had been successfully recovered through COGI.

However, only the MSPT status is cleared, while VMENG remains 0.

Is this the expected standard SAP behavior? If so, could you please point me to the relevant SAP Note or SAP Help documentation that explains this behavior?

COHV Execution Settings

The Collective Availability Check in COHV was executed with the following settings:

  • Function: 160 - Collective Availability Check
  • Execution Mode: Immediate
  • Processing Scope: ATP Check for All Components
  • Reset Availability Data: Not selected
  • Firm Planned Orders: Not selected
  • Log Output: None

Thank you in advance for your help.

entry.title: Forecasting in Commercial Project Management
entry.content:
The forecasting feature of Commercial Project Management (CPM) can be used by project managers to calculate cost and revenue estimates at the completion based on past performance, during the execution phase of projects. Forecasting allows you to arrive at estimates of the following key figures:

  • Estimate at completion (EAC) - Total projected cost, quantity, or revenue at project completion (Actuals + ETC).

  • Estimate to complete (ETC) - Projected remaining cost, quantity, or revenue from the forecast period to project completion.

Resource Types based on Plan/Forecast basis

There are four categories in which resource types can be categorized based on Plan/Forecast basis:

  1. Quantity - The Quantity, Rate, Cost (Rate) and Revenue (Rate) columns will be enabled for planning. Forecasted Cost and Forecasted Revenue are calculated based on Forecasted Quantity.
  2. Cost and Revenue - The Cost and Revenue columns will be enabled for planning.
  3. Revenue - Only the Revenue column will be enabled for planning.
  4. Cost - Only the Cost column will be enabled for planning.

In CPM, the following forecasting methods are used:

  • Forecast based on Actuals: This method is suitable for those projects which are time bound and flexible with respect to cost. The Actuals and remaining Plan derive the EAC for the project. The EAC is calculated by summing up actuals from the past periods and remaining planning for future periods.
  • Forecast based on Constant EAC: This method is suitable for those projects which are cost bound but flexible with respect to time. When the forecast is executed for the first time, the EAC will be copied as total plan value. The EAC remains constant throughout the forecast process until it is manually adjusted by changing ETC. In case the Actuals are not same as corresponding Planned values up to forecast period, the balanced value is adjusted to the future period. This adjustment is the difference between planned value up to forecast period and Actual value up to forecast period. If the adjustment is negative, then the ETC for the next period(s) will be reduced by this adjustment. Therefore ETC is re-calculated based on Actuals incurred while keeping the EAC constant.
  • Forecast based on Configuration: It is the dynamic forecasting method and is the combination of both the above methods. It is suitable for those projects where certain resources are time bound and others are cost bound. Each Resource Type can be configured to follow one of the above forecasting method.

Period Options in Forecasting

The following period options are available for forecasting:

  • In-Period Forecasting: Forecasting is performed at any time during an open period using the actuals posted up to that point, along with the remaining forecast for the rest of the period. Project Manager is allowed to adjust the forecast for current forecast period.

  • Period-End Forecasting: Forecasting is performed while closing the period, that is, when all actuals for that period have been posted. This provides a forecast based on complete actual data for the period.

There are different Planning Functions associated based on forecast method and the period option selected. These Planning Functions contain multiple formulae. Based on the above Resource types, the corresponding formula of the Planning Function will be executed. These formulae are part of BW IP Planning Function which gets executed when user executes the forecast. 

Each forecasting scenario is described in detail with examples in the following blogs:

  • In-Period and Period-End Forecasting based on Actuals
  • In-Period and Period-End Forecasting based on constant EAC

entry.title: How to review transfer history for an intra-company-code asset transfer (ABUML / F9302) - which App
entry.content:
Ref #315

System: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
Topic: Asset Accounting (FI-AA) - intra-company-code asset transfer

We perform intra-company-code asset transfers (e.g. from Asset A to Asset B)
using the following apps:

- Post Transfer - Within Company Code (ABUML) [current app]
- Post Asset Transfer (New Version) (F9302) [successor app, as of 2608,
business transaction type RA30 for transfer within company code]

Afterwards we would like to review the transfer history - ideally a list that
shows, per transfer, the posting date, the sending asset, the receiving asset
and the transferred amount, like this:

Posting Date | Transferred From | Transferred To | Amount
2026/07/24 | Asset A | Asset B | 100 USD

Questions:

1. Which app should be used to display this transfer history?
Is there a standard app that shows the sending and receiving asset side by
side in a single line, or do we need to read the sender and receiver
postings separately and match them by document number?

2. What are the exact steps in that app (selection criteria, filters, columns
to add) to obtain the above information?

3. Does the answer differ depending on whether the transfer was posted with
ABUML or with the successor app F9302? The 2608 What's New notes that the
separation of sender and receiver transaction line items is clearer in
F9302 - does this change how the transfer history can be displayed or
reported?

4. If no standard app provides this, is there a recommended CDS view or
analytical query that can be used instead?

Any pointer to the relevant SAP Help Portal documentation would be appreciated.

entry.title: From an Idea to Innovation: Our InnoJAM 2026 Journey
entry.content:
Turning a Support Challenge into a Vision for the Future

Innovation often starts with a simple observation: there must be a better way.

For our team of five, this journey began with identifying a challenge faced in Global Customer Support how we manage resolved and inactive support cases. What started as a discussion around reducing repetitive manual effort evolved into a vision of transforming case resolution through automation, knowledge reuse, and AI enablement.

Our idea, "Automatic Case Closure Upon Chat Resolution", focused on simplifying the post-resolution journey by automating case closure, capturing resolution knowledge in PULSE, and creating a foundation for future Autonomous Agent capabilities.

The Beginning: From Concept to Collaboration

As a team of five bringing together different perspectives, experiences, and ideas we started by understanding the current challenges faced by Support Engineers. After a chat resolution, significant manual effort is still required to update PULSE, maintain KBAs, assign error categories, add external information, and complete case closure activities.

We asked ourselves:

"What if every resolved case could automatically contribute to a smarter support ecosystem?"

This question became the foundation of our idea.

The InnoJAM Experience: Learning, Refining, and Challenging Our Thinking

The InnoJAM sessions with Sean played an important role in shaping our idea. The sessions helped us move beyond simply proposing automation and encouraged us to think about:

  • The real business problem we are solving.
  • The value created for customers and support teams.
  • How the idea can scale globally.
  • How it aligns with future AI-driven support strategies.

Through multiple discussions, we refined our approach from a simple case closure automation concept into a broader vision: transforming every resolved case into structured knowledge that can support continuous learning.

The feedback, discussions, and challenges raised during these sessions helped us strengthen our storytelling and focus on the impact rather than only the solution.

The Journey Through the Selection Rounds

Being selected from the regional rounds to progress to the final round was an incredible milestone for our team.

The moment we received confirmation that our idea had moved forward was both exciting and motivating. It validated the effort, creativity, and collaboration that went into developing the concept.

The preparation journey for the final round involved:

  • Refining our presentation flow.
  • Strengthening the business value.
  • Preparing for different perspectives and questions.
  • Ensuring we communicated not only the automation aspect but also the long-term AI vision.

The Final Presentation: Sharing Our Vision

Presenting to the jury was a valuable learning experience. The questions challenged us to think deeper about feasibility, scalability, and how our idea could integrate with existing capabilities.

The discussions highlighted an important point:

Innovation is not always about creating something completely new

 

 it is also about finding smarter ways to connect existing capabilities, simplify processes, and create greater value.

The jury discussions helped us see our idea from different angles and strengthened our understanding of how innovation needs both technology and business impact.

Beyond the Result: The Real Achievement

Although we did not win the final round, the journey itself was a tremendous achievement.

The experience gave us:

  • The opportunity to collaborate beyond our daily responsibilities.
  • Exposure to different innovation perspectives.
  • Confidence in presenting ideas at a global level.
  • A deeper understanding of how ideas evolve from concepts into scalable solutions.

The most rewarding part was the passion and contribution from everyone involved. The discussions, brainstorming sessions, and willingness to challenge each other's thinking made this experience truly memorable.

What We Take Forward

Innovation is a journey, not just a competition.

The InnoJAM experience reinforced that great ideas come from collaboration, curiosity, and the willingness to challenge existing processes.

Our Automatic Case Closure idea may have started as a solution to improve case lifecycle management, but the journey helped us discover a bigger vision—creating a smarter, knowledge-driven support ecosystem where every resolved customer interaction contributes to future success.

A special thank you to everyone who supported us, shared feedback, challenged our thinking, and encouraged us throughout this journey.

The biggest takeaway: the value of innovation is not only measured by the final outcome, but by the ideas created, the connections built, and the impact we continue to make.

entry.title: Re: Integration of Service Contract Templates in Service Contract Management
entry.content:
Hi @Wei 

Thank you for the blog on Service contract management .

I have one query related to management of additional information's in service contract .

Consider an equipment that contains 10 spare part categories (A to J). As per the service contract agreement, only spare part categories A and B are covered under the agreement, while categories C to J are billable.

Each spare part category (for example, A and B) can consist of multiple material numbers . In SAP CS, this requirement was addressed by assigning characteristics at the service contract item level, enabling the system to determine whether a Service material belonged to a covered spare part category. This will in turn reflect whether the spare part type is covered or not .

In SAP S/4HANA Service (SVC), is there a comparable design or standard functionality to achieve this? Specifically, how can multiple characteristics can be maintained in Service contract item level .

entry.title: Combined street field in DMEE ZCGI_XML_CT_V9
entry.content:
Hello,

Financial institutions have obliged ISO 20022 standard for bank files with structured address elements. As reaction, we have installed DMEE CGI_XML_CT_V9 to get bank file in pain.001.001.09 format.

By testing we have noticed that street name and house number are concatenated. We are expecting them to be in separate fields - street name should be in node <Cdtr><PstlAdr><StrtNm>; house number in node <Cdtr><PstlAdr><BldgNb>.

By debugging, we have seen that

In DMEE tree ZCGI_XML_CT_V9_UK, node <Cdtr><PstlAdr><StrtNm> uses exit function FI_CGI_DMEE_EXIT_W_BADI, which returns the value stored in FPAYHX-REF09, which is fetched from REGUH table - field ZSTRA 

Why does SAP standard do this and how can we solve it?

Best regards

entry.title: What's New in Sourcing and Procurement — SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

I am excited to share the latest innovations for Sourcing and Procurement from the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608 release. This update delivers six enhancements spanning AI-assisted procurement, supplier confirmation management, and purchase order output handling.

 

Supplier Confirmations

  • Attachments for Supplier Confirmation Business Object
  • Supplier Confirmation Notes into OData API

AI-Assisted Procurement

  • Goods and Services Receipt Agent

Purchase Order Management

  • Custom Field Enablement in Purchase Order Mass Maintenance
  • Display Output Request Items for Archived Purchase Order
  • Handling Output for Purchase Orders with MPN Material and Order Confirmation for MPN Material

What's New in Supplier Confirmations

Attachments for Supplier Confirmation Business Object

When managing supplier confirmations, supporting documents such as delivery notes, quality certificates, or packing lists often need to be stored alongside the confirmation record. Previously there was no dedicated place for these on the Supplier Confirmation business object itself — attachments had to be uploaded against the purchase order, making confirmation-specific documentation harder to track.

With 2608, attachments can now be uploaded directly on the Supplier Confirmation business object. All confirmation-related documents are centralized in one location, independent of the purchase order, improving traceability, transparency, and document management across the procurement process.

 

For more information, see: Attachments in Supplier Confirmations

Supplier Confirmation Notes into OData API

Supplier confirmation notes contain important information communicated by suppliers during the confirmation process — yet until now, purchasing teams and integration partners could only access these notes through the SAP Fiori UI, requiring manual tracking and making automated processing difficult.

With 2608, supplier confirmation notes are now exposed through the OData API. Notes are added to the relevant service binding, enabling customers to retrieve and use supplier confirmation notes programmatically through standard API access. This improves transparency and reduces manual effort for high-volume procurement teams.

 

For more information, see: What's New Viewer

What's New in AI-Assisted Procurement

Goods and Services Receipt Agent

For casual users who occasionally need to confirm goods receipt or service entry for self-service purchase requisitions, navigating into a dedicated SAP Fiori app adds unnecessary friction.

With 2608, users can query Joule to carry out confirmation of a self-service purchase requisition goods receipt or service entry conversationally, without navigating into a separate application. Joule guides the user through the confirmation in natural language, creating the goods receipt or service entry sheet directly. This removes a key barrier for casual users and accelerates the confirmation process.

 

For more information, see: What's New Viewer

What's New in Purchase Order Management

Custom Field Enablement in Purchase Order Mass Maintenance

Organizations using custom fields on purchase orders previously could not update those fields at scale using the Mass Changes to Purchase Orders application. Each custom field had to be updated individually, increasing effort and error risk for large-scale maintenance activities.

With 2608, customer-defined custom fields are now supported in the Mass Changes to Purchase Orders application. Purchasing teams can efficiently update custom field values across multiple purchase orders in a single operation, saving time and reducing manual effort.

 

For more information, see: Mass Changes to Purchase Orders

Display Output Request Items for Archived Purchase Order

Once a purchase order is archived, its associated output request items were previously inaccessible without restoring the archived document, creating gaps in audit trails and making compliance reporting more cumbersome.

With 2608, output request items remain accessible even after a purchase order is archived. Purchasing teams and auditors can view the full output history of an archived purchase order directly without restoration, strengthening document traceability and supporting compliance.

 

For more information, see: Output Messages for Archived Purchase Orders

Handling Output for Purchase Orders with MPN Material and Order Confirmation for MPN Material

For organizations sourcing MPN (Manufacturer Part Number) materials, purchase orders and order confirmations must clearly communicate both the internal material number and the manufacturer-specific identifier. Without this, suppliers may face ambiguity about exactly which manufacturer part is being ordered.

With 2608, complete manufacturer details including Manufacturer, Manufacturer Part Number, and Manufacturer Name are now clearly displayed on purchase order output and order confirmations for MPN materials. This eliminates ambiguity, fosters stronger vendor collaboration, and reduces procurement errors for businesses handling complex MPN sourcing scenarios.

 

For more information, see: What's New Viewer

Stay Connected

Do not hesitate to leave a comment and check out our community page to ask your questions and engage with the experts. Follow the PSCC_Enablement tag to stay up to date with our latest blog posts.

More innovation can be found in the What's New Viewer.

Watch the replay of our SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608 Early Release Series!

From July 13-17, we hosted expert-led live sessions to highlight the exciting innovations shipped with the 2608 release. Watch the Sourcing and Procurement session on demand — anywhere, anytime.

entry.title: Creating new tax codes in SSCUI 101016 – Starter system vs. Development system (Japan / TDT)
entry.content:
Title: Creating new tax codes in SSCUI 101016 – Starter system vs. Development system (Japan / TDT)

Hi experts,

We are implementing SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition for a Japanese company code and need to create additional tax codes for Japan (JP).

Background:
Japan requires new tax codes for the transitional measures of the qualified invoice system (purchases from non-registered suppliers). The SAP Help page "Processing Value Added Tax – Japan" states that in Japan new tax codes must be defined for transitional measures. Our predecessor ECC system has custom tax codes (10% and reduced 8% for purchases from tax-exempt businesses), and the deduction ratio is scheduled to change under the FY2026 tax reform, so we expect to need tax codes that are not part of the pre-delivered set (N2–N7).

We are currently in the Explore phase and only have the Starter system. The Development system has not been provisioned yet.

Questions:
1) In the Starter system, is it possible at all to create a new tax code via SSCUI 101016 (Define Tax Codes for Sales and Purchases)? We opened the SSCUI but cannot find how to add a new tax code. No error message is displayed. Is creation simply not supported in the Starter system, or are we missing something in the UI?

2) Once the Development system is provisioned, will tax code creation be possible there via CBC / SSCUI 101016?

3) If yes, what are the prerequisites and constraints? For example:
- Required business role / authorization (SAP_BR_BPC_EXPERT?)
- Any TDT-related prerequisites for Japan
- Restrictions on the tax code ID or tax type
- Follow-up configuration required (automatic account determination, tax box mapping, etc.)
- Anything specific to maintaining both the deductible and non-deductible portions for partially deductible input tax

4) Is there any recommended step-by-step guidance beyond KBA 3009061? The steps there do not seem to match what we see in our system.

Environment: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, 3-system landscape (planned), country/region JP, TDT enabled for Japan.

Any guidance would be much appreciated.

Thanks in advance.

entry.title: Re: COHV Availability Check clears MSPT but does not upda...
entry.content:
Hello @darien 

Your scenario steps don't contain any reference to COHV availability check. Thus, it's all but impossible if or when you execute availability check.

In general, availability check of production order components should be done before release, to make sure you have enough components to complete the order. It looks like you don't follow that - you have COGI for unavailable components and you reprocess COGI when the components are available. I don't see where COHV availability check sits in your scenario.

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: Standard API to Maintain Dunning Level and Last Dunned During AR-to-AR Reclassification
entry.content:
We are performing an AR-to-AR reclassification in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

We currently use either:

  • the Journal Entry - Post (Asynchronous) API, or
  • Upload Customer Open Items,

However, neither option allows us to provide or preserve the following dunning information:

  • Dunning Level
  • Last Dunned

These fields can be maintained manually in the Manage Customer Line Items app, but for a large number of documents this is not feasible.

Could you please confirm whether there is any standard API, import object, mass upload functionality, or any other supported interface that allows us to create customer open items including the Dunning Level and Last Dunned fields?

Our goal is to preserve the dunning status during an AR-to-AR reclassification without requiring manual maintenance after the posting.

entry.title: How to built rule for leave eligibility using a rolling 12-month period from first leave applied?
entry.content:
Business Requirement

Leave Type: Unpaid Leave

Entitlement: The maximum number of leave days is determined by the employee's assigned work schedule (working days per week).

  • Example:
    • 3 working days/week = 3 unpaid leave days per rolling 12 months.
    • 5 working days/week = 5 unpaid leave days per rolling 12 months.

Evaluation Period:

  • Rolling 12-month period starting from the employee's first approved Unpaid Leave request, not a calendar year or fixed leave year.
  • The first approved leave request establishes the start of the employee's personal 12-month window.
  • All subsequent Unpaid Leave requests within that 12-month window must be validated against the employee's maximum entitlement.
  • If the cumulative approved leave days within that rolling period exceed the employee's eligible limit, the leave request should be rejected with an appropriate error message.
  • Once the 12-month period expires, a new 12-month window begins based on the next approved leave request.

Example:

 

  • Employee work schedule = 3 working days/week.
  • Maximum Unpaid Leave entitlement = 3 days.
  • First approved Unpaid Leave = 12 July 2026.
  • Rolling period = 12 July 2026 to 11 July 2027.
  • Employee can take a total of 3 days during this period.
  • If another request causes the total to exceed 3 days, the system should reject the request.
  • After 11 July 2027, the next approved Unpaid Leave starts a new rolling 12-month period with a fresh entitlement of 3 days.

I would appreciate any help or insights regarding the requirement above. If you have any suggestions, recommendations, or see any potential issues or considerations, please share your thoughts.

Thank you in advance for your time and support.

Best Regards,

Venkat

entry.title: Making AI Real: Promocean
entry.content:
When it comes to AI, there are so many possibilities that it can be hard to know where to start. And that’s why real-world examples of AI in production are so valuable. Here’s a great one from the 2026 SAP Innovation Awards: Promocean, who managed to get value from AI in their existing ECC environment, while future-proofing a move to S/4HANA at some point.

Imagine you’re a world-class brand like Heineken, Lindt chocolates, Bacardi, or Lacoste, and you’re organizing a promotional event. You want the event to feel special, different, creative, but still 100% aligned with your brand image — and of course for the right price, delivered on time. That’s when you might turn to Promocean, the winner of the Technology Pathfinder category of the 2026 SAP Innovation Awards. 

The Dutch company is trusted by some of the top brands in the world to create award-winning point of sale marketing materials and packaging. 

Procurement is crucial for Promocean because they need to efficiently source very customized materials and services from around the world, while meeting strict cost, quality, and delivery requirements. 

Surveys show that communication between vendors and buyers is a major source of frustration in almost every industry. Promocean had an overloaded team spending up to 19 hours a day on purchase order follow-ups, using manual processes based on email and excel that just didn’t scale. 

In addition, supply chain delays weren’t as visible as they should be, there was no easy way to track the reliability of different vendors. and when issues did come up, it was hard to collaborate across different internal teams.

At a business AI hackathon organized late last year by SAP Netherlands and the Dutch SAP user group, VNSG, Promocean saw an opportunity to use AI to reduce these risks and inefficiencies. Working with their long-term strategic consulting partner SOA PEOPLE and an SAP expert coach, they designed and implemented an AI-driven procurement solution powered by SAP Business AI Platform. 

The way it works is interesting. First, there’s the design phase using AI. Promocean had a big advantage — they had already thoroughly documented what the process should look like. Then using the SAP AI platform, they fed that into a GPT model using a now-standard RAG approach, retrieval augmented generation, and used it to create a very sophisticated and complex rules-based engine for handling different types of enquiries. 

Then there’s the operational phase: an unattended bot using SAP Build Process Automation gets emails from Outlook every 30 minutes. Those are then interpreted using large language models from Mistral and Anthropic on SAP’s GenAI Hub and enriched with back-end data from an on-premise SAP ECC system — for example looking up the current status of an order or invoice.

The email summary is then passed onto the rules engine to determine the best next step, and generate intelligent action proposals. Users can then review and manage these proposals in real-time, using the very latest information available, using a custom Purchase Order Control Center built with Fiori and the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model in SAP Build Work Zone.

The final choice is always with the end-user. If they accept the action, the next steps can be carried out automatically by the system. If they disagree with the proposed solution, that feedback is fed back into the system to improve it over time. 

The solution went live on January 19th 2026 after less than 20 weeks of development, resulting in more automated, timely, and consistent follow-up of purchase orders — reducing manual effort by 63% and with fewer errors. The team estimates that by leveraging AI techniques they were able to reduce development effort by 90%! There’s faster and more consistent vendor follow-up as the team is able to move from repetitive operational work to focus on what’s important.  And the system is scalable and future-proof, a platform for other AI projects, providing value today while being ready for Promocean’s move to S/4 HANA at some point in the future. 

Lessons learned:

  • Look for opportunities to participate in hackathons and design workshops with your strategic vendors and partners, to find the best. most feasible AI projects for your organization, and make sure you involve the employees concerned, to tap into their knowledge and excitement.
  • Real-world AI works best when it’s built on top of a robust foundation, like SAP Business AI platform, that helps you move from prototype to production, and prepare for the AI future.

You can get more details about the solution in Promocean’s SAP Innovation Award entry and inin this video from Twan van den Broek:

At a business AI hackathon organized late last year by SAP Netherlands and the Dutch SAP user group, VNSG, Promocean saw an opportunity to use AI to reduce these risks and inefficiencies. Working with their long-term strategic consulting partner SOA PEOPLE and an SAP expert coach, they designed and implemented an AI-driven procurement solution powered by SAP Business AI Platform.

entry.title: Bulk Upload of Inspection Results in SAP
entry.content:
We need a way to upload inspection results for serialized materials in bulk in SAP.

The standard "Record Inspection Results in Table form" app allows users to record inspection results, but it does not support uploading results for multiple inspection lots from an Excel file. Therefore, users must enter the inspection results manually for each inspection lot.

Each inspection lot contains 20 to 50 serial numbers. Even when the inspection result is the same for all serial numbers, users must enter the same result separately for each serial number. This makes the process slow and time-consuming.

Is there a standard SAP solution to upload inspection results in bulk from an Excel file? If not, is custom development required? If custom development is needed, please provide the recommended approach and the step-by-step process.

entry.title: Re: Mastering SAP Datasphere Replication Scheduling
entry.content:
@nilrod do you mean events in S/4? or in other systems? It is now possible to schedule via API Managing Tasks via the REST API 

What I'm really missing is a way to schedule depending on plant calendars like you can do in S/4. E.g. load more frequent the first 4 workdays of a month.

@yogen_patil I think that you miss quite a lot by only looking at the technical differences here, they kind of doesn't matter that much. It's much more important to understand the differences between "Frequency-Based Scheduling" and the Scheduled runs in terms of in which context they can be used and which limitations it gives.

Frequency based is more mature technically e.g. it allows you to stop a replication flow (which will clean up on the source side). If you choose to schedule (simple or cron) then you can't stop a replication flow. The frequency is a number of hours / minutes / days after the last job terminates i.e. there is no risk of overlapping loads.

Scheduled jobs on the other hand can be started in task chains and you can thereby start related jobs when the replication has finished, which is not possible when using frequency based schedules. On the negative side you can start replication flows that are all ready running which will cause your chain to fail, i.e. you will have to consider more error handling.

With kind regards

Kristian
